title: Anger at a Mentor Who Took Credit
dek: When the person who taught you how to stand in your power uses it to elevate themselves, the betrayal lives in the fascia. Here is how to metabolize the fury of being erased by someone you trusted.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight of the Stolen Word

You are sitting in the audience, or perhaps you are reading an email, or maybe you are scrolling through a feed, and there it is. The phrase you coined. The framework you built in the quiet hours of the morning. The methodology you bled for, synthesized from your own lived experience and countless hours of study. And it is coming out of the mouth, or the keyboard, of the person who was supposed to guide you. Your mentor. The one who held the lantern while you found your footing. 

Notice what happens in your body in this exact moment. Do not rush past it. Do not immediately jump to the rationalization that "ideas belong to the collective" or "imitation is the highest form of flattery." Feel the biological reality of the betrayal. It usually starts in the gut—a sudden, icy drop, a vacuum opening up just below your diaphragm. Then, the heat. The flush of blood rushing to your face, the tightening of the masseter muscles in your jaw. Your shoulders creep up toward your ears, the trapezius and levator scapulae locking into a defensive posture. 

This is not a moral failure on your part. This is not a sign that you are unevolved or lacking in spiritual maturity. This is biology. Your body is registering a threat to your sovereignty, a theft of your energetic output. Anger, in this moment, is the most appropriate, healthy, and vital response you could possibly have. It is your body's way of saying, *I exist. I matter. What I create has value.*

When a mentor takes credit for your work, the betrayal is uniquely complex. This is someone you trusted, someone who held a position of power and authority in your nervous system. You likely projected archetypal qualities onto them—the wise elder, the protector, the guide. When that person violates the implicit contract of mentorship, the cognitive dissonance is staggering. Your mind struggles to reconcile the image of the benevolent teacher with the reality of the opportunistic thief. 

But your body does not struggle with this reconciliation. Your body knows exactly what happened. The psoas muscle, the deep core stabilizer that connects your spine to your legs, tightens in preparation for fight or flight. Your breath becomes shallow, trapped in the upper chest. You might feel a sudden, overwhelming exhaustion—the result of your nervous system slamming on the brakes to contain the explosive energy of the anger.

## The Anatomy of Betrayal

To understand the depth of this anger, we must look at the somatic architecture of mentorship. When we enter into a mentoring relationship, we are inherently in a vulnerable position. We are opening ourselves up to be seen, to be shaped, to be influenced. We are extending a tendril of attachment, seeking safety and validation from someone further along the path. 

Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20), illuminates how trauma and betrayal are stored not just in our memories, but in our very physiology. When a trusted figure violates that trust, the body records it as a profound shock. The nervous system, which had previously categorized this person as "safe," must rapidly reclassify them as "dangerous." This sudden shift requires an immense amount of biological energy. 

The anger you feel is the fuel for this reclassification. It is the fire that burns away the illusion of safety, allowing you to see the reality of the situation. But because this person is a mentor, society—and often the mentor themselves—will try to gaslight you out of your anger. They will use spiritualized language to bypass the harm. They will talk about "co-creation" and "shared resonance." They will suggest that your anger is a sign of your own unhealed ego.

Do not swallow this poison. When you swallow your anger, it does not disappear. It simply turns inward. It becomes the tension headache that wakes you up at 3:00 AM. It becomes the chronic digestive issue, the sudden intolerance to foods you used to love. It becomes a generalized anxiety, a low-grade hum of hypervigilance that colors every interaction. 

Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma is not just an event, but a retention of energy in the body. When we are wronged and we cannot express the corresponding defensive response, that energy gets stuck in our tissues. The anger at your mentor, if left unexpressed, will calcify in your fascia. It will restrict your movement, both physically and creatively. You will find yourself hesitating before sharing your ideas, second-guessing your own brilliance, shrinking yourself to avoid being stolen from again.

## Metabolizing the Fury

So, how do we move this energy? How do we metabolize the fury of being erased by someone we trusted? We do not do it by forgiving them prematurely. We do not do it by sending them "love and light." We do it by honoring the anger, by giving it space to breathe and move and speak.

First, we must ground ourselves in the physical reality of the emotion. When the anger flares up, notice where it lives in your body. Is it a burning sensation in your chest? A tightness in your throat? A buzzing in your hands? Do not try to change it. Just observe it. Breathe into it. Let it be as big and as loud as it needs to be.

Herbal allies can be profoundly supportive in this process. [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a powerful adaptogen that helps the nervous system process stress and trauma. It does not numb the anger; rather, it gives your body the resilience to hold the intensity of the emotion without burning out. It supports the adrenal glands, which are often depleted by the chronic stress of betrayal.

Similarly, [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe the digestive tract, which is often the first system to go offline when we are in a state of fight or flight. The bitter principles in chamomile stimulate the vagus nerve, signaling to the body that it is safe to rest and digest, even in the midst of emotional turmoil.

But herbs alone are not enough. We must also engage in somatic practices that allow the trapped energy to complete its cycle. 

### An Embodied Practice for Reclaiming Your Energy

Find a quiet space where you will not be disturbed. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the solid ground beneath you. 

Bring your awareness to your core, to the space just below your navel. Imagine a fire burning there. This is your creative life force, your sovereignty, your brilliance. 

Now, visualize the mentor who took credit for your work. See them standing in front of you. Notice any cords or attachments connecting your energy to theirs. 

Take a deep breath in, drawing energy up from the earth into your core. As you exhale, make a sharp, forceful "HA!" sound, and simultaneously make a cutting motion with your hands, severing the cords between you and the mentor. 

Repeat this several times. With each "HA!", feel yourself reclaiming your energy, pulling it back into your own body. Feel the boundaries of your physical form becoming solid and impermeable. 

When you feel complete, place your hands over your heart and your belly. Breathe deeply, feeling the fullness of your own presence. Say out loud: *My ideas belong to me. My brilliance belongs to me. I am sovereign in my creation.*

## The Grief Beneath the Anger

As the acute heat of the anger begins to dissipate, you will likely encounter the grief that lies beneath it. This is the sorrow of the broken illusion. It is the mourning for the mentor you thought you had, the relationship you thought was safe. 

Pema Chödrön speaks eloquently about the concept of *shenpa*, the hook that catches us and pulls us into habitual patterns of reaction. The betrayal by a mentor is a massive hook. It triggers all of our deepest insecurities about our worth, our intelligence, our right to exist. 

The grief is the process of unhooking. It is the painful realization that the person we looked up to is flawed, perhaps deeply narcissistic, and ultimately incapable of holding our brilliance without trying to consume it. 

Allow yourself to grieve. Cry the tears that need to be cried. Feel the ache in your chest, the heaviness in your limbs. This grief is a testament to the depth of your capacity for connection and trust. It is not a weakness; it is a measure of your humanity.

Do not rush the grieving process. It takes time for the nervous system to recalibrate, for the body to learn that it can trust again—starting with trusting itself. 

## Rebuilding the Architecture of Trust

The ultimate resolution of this anger is not necessarily confrontation, though that may be appropriate in some cases. The ultimate resolution is the reclamation of your own authority. 

When a mentor takes credit for your work, they are attempting to assert dominance, to position themselves as the source of the wisdom. Your anger is the rejection of that false hierarchy. It is the recognition that the wisdom came through you, from your own deep connection to the source.

As you metabolize the anger and process the grief, you will begin to rebuild the architecture of trust within yourself. You will learn to recognize the subtle somatic cues that indicate when someone is not safe, when a relationship is extractive rather than reciprocal. 

You will become more discerning about who you share your ideas with, not out of fear, but out of a deep respect for the value of your own creations. You will learn to set boundaries that are not rigid walls, but flexible, responsive membranes that protect your energy while allowing for genuine connection.

And most importantly, you will step into your own mentorship. You will become the guide you needed. You will hold space for others with integrity, honoring their contributions and celebrating their brilliance without feeling threatened by it. 

The anger at the mentor who took credit is a crucible. It burns away the naive reliance on external authority and forges a fierce, unshakeable trust in your own inner knowing. It is a painful initiation, but it is also a profound graduation. You are no longer the student. You are the master of your own domain.

## The Somatic Reality of Forgiveness

Society places a heavy burden on the wronged to forgive. We are told that holding onto anger is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to die. But this cliché fundamentally misunderstands the nature of anger and the process of forgiveness.

Forgiveness is not a cognitive decision. You cannot simply decide to forgive someone and have it be so. Forgiveness is a somatic event. It is the natural byproduct of a nervous system that has fully processed the trauma, metabolized the anger, and integrated the grief. 

When you try to force forgiveness before the body is ready, you are committing an act of violence against yourself. You are overriding your own biological truth in order to conform to a spiritualized ideal. This forced forgiveness is often just a sophisticated form of dissociation. It allows you to float above the pain, but it leaves the trauma trapped in your tissues.

True forgiveness happens when the body no longer needs the anger to protect itself. It happens when the boundaries have been re-established, when the energy has been reclaimed, when the nervous system feels safe again. 

You will know you have reached this point not because you feel a sudden rush of love for the mentor who betrayed you, but because you feel a profound neutrality. The thought of them no longer triggers a spike in your heart rate or a tightening in your gut. They simply become a person who did a harmful thing, a character in a chapter of your life that has now closed.

Until that somatic neutrality arises naturally, your job is not to forgive. Your job is to feel. Your job is to honor the anger, to protect your energy, and to fiercely advocate for your own worth.

## The Legacy of the Stolen Idea

What happens to the idea that was stolen? Does it lose its power because it is being spoken by someone else? 

In the short term, it can feel that way. It can feel like your intellectual property has been contaminated, tainted by the ego of the person who took it. But ideas have their own life force. They are not static objects; they are living, breathing entities that evolve and adapt.

When a mentor takes your idea and presents it as their own, they are usually only capturing the surface level of it. They do not have the deep, somatic understanding of the concept because they did not birth it. They are wearing your clothes, but the clothes do not fit them properly. 

Your true power lies not in the single idea that was taken, but in the fertile ground from which that idea sprang. You are the source. You are the wellspring. The mentor may have stolen a bucket of water, but you own the entire aquifer. 

As you continue to create, to synthesize, to birth new concepts and frameworks, the stolen idea will become a mere footnote in your body of work. Your voice, grounded in your authentic lived experience, will resonate with a depth and a truth that cannot be faked or stolen. 

The anger you feel now is the fierce protector of that wellspring. It is the guardian at the gate, ensuring that you never again give away your power so freely. Honor it. Listen to it. Let it guide you back to yourself.

## FAQ

**Q: Should I confront the mentor who took credit for my work?**
**A:** There is no one-size-fits-all answer to this. Confrontation can be empowering, but it can also be re-traumatizing if the mentor is defensive, gaslighting, or narcissistic. Before deciding, check in with your body. Does the idea of confronting them make you feel expansive and grounded, or contracted and panicked? If you do choose to confront them, do it for your own closure, not with the expectation that they will apologize or change. Your goal is to speak your truth and reclaim your energy, regardless of their response.

**Q: How do I stop obsessing over the injustice of it all?**
**A:** Obsessive thinking is often a sign that the energy of the anger is trapped in the mind and hasn't been allowed to move through the body. When you find yourself ruminating, shift your attention from the story in your head to the sensations in your body. Where do you feel the injustice physically? Breathe into that space. Engage in vigorous physical activity—running, dancing, punching a pillow—to help move the adrenaline and cortisol out of your system. The obsession will naturally fade as the physical energy is discharged.

**Q: I feel like I can never trust another teacher or mentor again. Is this normal?**
**A:** Absolutely. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it is designed to do: protecting you from further harm. Do not try to force yourself to trust again before you are ready. Instead, focus on building trust with yourself. Practice listening to your own intuition, honoring your own boundaries, and validating your own experiences. As your self-trust deepens, you will naturally develop the discernment to recognize safe, reciprocal relationships in the future.

**Q: What if the mentor is highly respected in our community and no one will believe me?**
**A:** This is one of the most painful aspects of this type of betrayal. The power dynamic often means that the mentor controls the narrative. It is crucial to find at least one or two safe people—friends, a therapist, a support group—who can validate your experience and hold the truth with you. You do not need the entire community to believe you in order to heal. You only need to know the truth yourself, and to have it witnessed by those who truly matter.

**Q: Can I still use the idea or framework that they stole?**
**A:** Yes. It is yours. Reclaim it. Speak about it, write about it, teach it with the depth and nuance that only the true creator can provide. Over time, the authenticity of your voice will outshine the hollow echo of their imitation. Do not let their theft silence you. Let it be the catalyst that forces you to speak louder and clearer than ever before.


The physical toll of this kind of betrayal cannot be overstated. When we are forced to swallow our truth, the body bears the burden. The vagus nerve, which regulates our parasympathetic nervous system, becomes dysregulated. We may experience heart palpitations, shallow breathing, or a persistent feeling of dread in the pit of the stomach. This is the body's alarm system, blaring loudly, demanding that we pay attention to the boundary violation that has occurred. It is essential to listen to these somatic signals, rather than trying to medicate them away or spiritualize them into non-existence. The body is the ultimate truth-teller, and its wisdom must be honored if true healing is to take place.

Furthermore, the impact on our creative expression is profound. The throat chakra, the energetic center of communication and truth, often becomes blocked. We may find ourselves literally unable to speak, our voices catching in our throats when we try to articulate what happened. This is a protective mechanism, a way for the body to prevent further exposure and vulnerability. To heal this, we must gently coax the voice back out, starting with small, safe expressions of truth. Singing, chanting, or simply humming can help to vibrate the vocal cords and release the trapped energy in the throat. Over time, as the nervous system begins to feel safe again, the voice will return, stronger and more resonant than before.
